Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 
Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied: 

Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 
Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target sellers is: 


Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

See PowerPoint for solution.  0.5 pt for getting market-clearing prices for each submarket. 0.5pt for noticing that non-observers will deviate. 1pt for noticing that only situation C “works”. 0.5pt for the general rule.

In the meantime, the supply-chain problems and the Ukrainian war has led to an inward shift of the supply. Suppose the supply becomes:  What is the new equilibrium price and what is the new equilibrium quantity?

	•	High gas prices have imposed a great burden on consumers. Suppose that the government now wishes to protect consumers by taxing gasoline supply and considers to impose a $0.5 tax per gallon on gas suppliers. What is the equilibrium price and quantity after tax?


	•	Calculate the burden of this proposed tax; what percent of the tax would be “paid” by consumers (Round to nearest percent)? How is this related to your answer in part c)?
Price increase of $0.33 out of $0.5 tax, == 67%. From part c), the demand curve is less elastic than the supply curve at the equilibrium so consumers have more tax burden.


	•	What is the amount of deadweight loss created by this policy? Is this policy a good idea?

Deadweight loss=0.5*0.5*16.67=4.16.
Taxing gas suppliers may not be a good idea consumers end up paying more. Even if the government rebates all tax revenue, there is still a deadweight loss of $4.16M

Problem 3. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber
Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber is given by

where is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound. Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is given by 

where  is US output in millions of pounds and p is the domestic price per pound. 
	•	Solve for the domestic equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.

	•	Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound. There is enough availability that the US can import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? What would be the amount imported? 

□ Yes, US would import      □ No, US would not import
Quantity imported: ________________________
Solution: 
Yes, US would import. 
US demand at world price of $9 is 40-2*9= 22
The domestic supply at price of $9 is 2P/3 = 6.
Therefore, quantity imported = 16.

	•	In recent years, the US government imposed a $3 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?

US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________
Ans: $12
	•	What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?
Tariff revenue: ________________________
Ans: Import when price=12: 16-8=8. Tariff revenue=3*8=24. 
	•	Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers from such a tariff? 
Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information

□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information

□ Increase

Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________

Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce deadweight loss. 

	•	(Challenge question:) Under pressure from trading partners, the US is considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US to 8 million pounds a year. Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the domestic price of vegetable fiber? What will be the amount produced by domestic producers? 

Answer. 
To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds). So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, the effective domestic supply becomes Qs=8+2/3p
Domestic price: _____________________
Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________

40-2P=8+2/3PP=12 
Domestic Q=8

All three risk types are equally represented in the population, and all people have an initial wealth level of $100K, and a utility function over wealth of  (where  is measured in thousands of dollars). To be clear, a HIGH risk person therefore has initial wealth of $100K and a 95% chance of $0 medical expenses, but a 5% chance of $100K in medical expenses.

Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. yf (pf) = 50 − (1/5)pf

To get the aggregate inverse demand, the first step is to identify for what price range Apple will sell only to firms (the more inelastic consumers) and for what price range it will serve both firms and individuals. The two demand functions are 
pi  = 62.5 − (5/4) Yi (pi )
pf = 250 – 5 yf (pf)

above a price of 62.5, Apple will only serve to firms and its inverse demand function will be:
Y(p) = 50 − (1/5)p

Y(p) = Yi (p ) + Yf (p ) = 100 – p.

	•	(1 point) Find the level of sales, price, and profit in this scenario (no price discrimination). 

If Apple only sells to firms, its demand is p(y) = 250 – 5y and marginal revenues are MR(y) = 250 -10y. Since MC = 0, y = 25 and p = 125. Profits will be 3,125.

If Apple sells to both individuals and firms, its demand is p(y) = 100 – y and marginal revenues are MR(y) = 100 -2y. Since MC = 0, y = 50 and p = 50. Profits will be 2,500. This profit level is lower than what Apple would get by only selling to firms, so it will choose to set p = 125, sell y = 25 only to firms, and obtain a profit of 3,125.

Sales: 25
Price: 125
Profit: 3,125

	•	(1.5 points) Compare producer's and consumer's surplus in the three cases: uniform price, perfect discrimination, and 3rd-degree price discrimination.

Uniform price: With a demand of p = 100 – y, MR(y) = 100 – 2y, y = 50, and p = 50. Profits (which are equivalent to the producer surplus) are equal to 2,500. Consumer surplus is the triangle between the price level (50) and the demand curve, and can be calculated as (100-50) [the height of the triangle] x 50 [the quantity] x 0.5 = 1,250.
Perfect discrimination: Producer surplus is 5,000, consumer surplus is 0 (see point 1).
3rd degree price discrimination: 
Apple will cater to each individual market segment separately, choosing different prices and quantities. We already know from point 3 that it will charge firms a price p=125, sell y = 25, and make a profit/producer surplus of 3,125. Consumer surplus is (62.5-31.25) x 25 x 0.5 = 390.625. So as a whole, under 3rd degree price discrimination, profits are equal to 3,125 + 781.25 = 3,906.25, consumer surplus is 390.625 + 1562.5 = 1,953.125.

Problem 6: Extended location games [25 points]
Recall the location choice game discussed in the class: Tourists are evenly distributed on a beach that is one mile long. Two ice cream vendors simultaneously choose where to locate, each aiming to maximize their own market share. (Suppose they can locate exactly at the same spot if they want.) Tourists prefer to buy from the closer vendor. When they are indifferent between vendors, they randomly choose one. In the following, we consider a few variants of this location choice game.
Suppose that there are now THREE ice cream vendors.
(a) [5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that they all locate in the middle of the beach? Please explain your answer.

Problem 7: Investment game [20 points]
Consider the following sequential-move game: firm 1 first decides whether to adopt an aggressive low-price policy or an accommodating high-price policy; after seeing firm 1’s choice, firm 2 decides whether or not to invest in a new technology which reduces its production cost. The investment cost is k.
If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, then firm 1 will earn 5, and firm 2 will have no business (e.g., because its production cost is too high) and earn 0. If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 invests to reduce its production cost, then firm 1 earns 1 and firm 2 earns 4 (before considering its investment cost). If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, firm 1 will earn 4, and firm 2 will have some business and earn 2. If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 invests, then firm 1 will have no business and earn 0, and firm 2 will earn 5 (before considering its investment cost).
(a) [5 points] Draw the game tree of this sequential-move game. [Hint: payoffs are net profit, so don’t forget the investment cost.]


(b) [10 points] For what values of k, will firm 1 choose to set a high price in the beginning?
k should be between 3 and 4.
Two conditions should be satisfied for firm 1 to set a high price: First, when firm 1 sets a high price, firm 2 should respond by choosing not to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 0, which must be worse than choosing a low price.) This requires 5-k<2, or k>3. Second, when firm 1 sets a low price, firm 2 should respond by choosing to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 5, in which case it wouldn’t choose a high price.) This requires 4-k>0, or k<4.
(c) [5 points] Suppose the investment cost increases from k=2.5 to k=3.5. Will firm 2 suffer or benefit from that? Please explain your answer.
Firm 2 benefits from the increase of k. 
When k=2.5, firm 1 will set a low price, and then firm 2 will invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 1.5. When k=3.5, firm 1 will set a high price, and then firm 2 will not invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 2. (A higher investment cost induces firm 1 to be less aggressive in the beginning.)

 X≥3 and Y≥7 (or X>3 and Y>7). [3 points for each value.] 

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have a unique NE that predicts the same outcome as in the sequential-move version of this game, where player 1 moves first? [6 points]
[Hint: find all possible values of X and Y; try to use backward induction without drawing the game tree for the sequential game.]

There are two possible cases where the simultaneous game has a unique NE: (X≥3, Y<7) in which case the NE is (T, C), and (X<3, Y≥7) in which case the NE is (B, R).

In the sequential-move game:
if player 1 chooses T, then player 2 will choose C, so player 1 will get X;
if player 1 chooses M, then player 2 will choose L, so player 1 will get 6;
if player 1 chooses B, then player 2 will choose L or R, depending on whether Y<7 or Y>7, so player 1 will get 2 or 4. In either case the outcome is worse for player 1 than 6 when she chooses M.
Therefore, player 1 will NEVER choose B. The outcome is either (T, C) if X>6 or (M, L) if X<6.

Therefore, to make the simultaneous and the sequential game have the same (unique) prediction, the only possibility is that (T, C) happens in either case. 

For (T, C) to be the unique NE in the simultaneous game, as pointed out before, we need (X≥3, Y<7); for the same outcome to happen in the sequential game, we need X>6. Therefore, the condition we are seeking is (X>6, Y<7).

Consider the following simultaneous-move game with two unknown payoff numbers X and Y. X≠3, Y≠7, and they are integers.

	•	If X=5 and Y=8, does any player have a dominant or dominated strategy? [3 points]

Player 1 has a dominated strategy M.

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have two Nash equilibria (NE)? [6 points] [Hint: find all possible values of X and Y, and express your answers in inequalities.]

 X≥3 and Y≥7 (or X>3 and Y>7). [3 points for each value.] 

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have a unique NE that predicts the same outcome as in the sequential-move version of this game, where player 1 moves first? [6 points]
[Hint: find all possible values of X and Y; try to use backward induction without drawing the game tree for the sequential game.]

X>6 and Y<7. [3 points for each value] (Give full points if they use weak inequalities.)

There are two possible cases where the simultaneous game has a unique NE: (X≥3, Y<7) in which case the NE is (T, C), and (X<3, Y≥7) in which case the NE is (B, R).

In the sequential-move game:
if player 1 chooses T, then player 2 will choose C, so player 1 will get X;
if player 1 chooses M, then player 2 will choose L, so player 1 will get 6;
if player 1 chooses B, then player 2 will choose L or R, depending on whether Y<7 or Y>7, so player 1 will get 2 or 4. In either case the outcome is worse for player 1 than 6 when she chooses M.
Therefore, player 1 will NEVER choose B. The outcome is either (T, C) if X>6 or (M, L) if X<6.

Therefore, to make the simultaneous and the sequential game have the same (unique) prediction, the only possibility is that (T, C) happens in either case. 

For (T, C) to be the unique NE in the simultaneous game, as pointed out before, we need (X≥3, Y<7); for the same outcome to happen in the sequential game, we need X>6. Therefore, the condition we are seeking is (X>6, Y<7).

Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $105,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 105,000.

The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 105,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $82,500


	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral, they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least one between w and b would have to be higher.
	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $80,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $80,000 is high enough and P4P is better off offering the the bonus scheme.


Game theory long questions (35 points)

Problem 4: Price Competition and Capacity [20 points]
Consider a one-shot price competition between Apple and Samsung for their new smartphones. Each company can chose to charge either $800 or $1,000. The following is the DEMAND matrix (not the profit matrix):

For example, if both firms set a price of $800, Apple will have a demand of 30 million units and Samsung will have a demand of 20 million units. (While the demand numbers are in Millions, you don’t need to account for that in your calculations.) Suppose the production cost per unit is $400, the same for both companies. (We don’t consider fixed costs as they usually don’t impact price competition.) Assume each company aims to maximize its own profits.
a) Suppose first that the two companies set their prices simultaneously. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by constructing the game matrix with profit numbers. Don’t forget to include costs, and be sure to double check your calculations before proceeding.]
(a-1) Does Samsung have a dominant strategy? If so, please specify. [4 points]
(a-2) What prices will the two companies set in the Nash Equilibrium? [4 points]

b) Suppose now that Apple announces its price first, and then Samsung responds. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by drawing the game tree.]
(b-1) What prices will the two companies set? [4 points]
(b-2) If Samsung announces its price first, how will each company’s profit change? [4 points] 
c) Return to the simultaneous-move game, but now suppose Apple has a capacity constraint of 20 million units, while Samsung does not. In other words, Apple cannot serve more than 20 million customers, and any unserved customers will buy from Samsung. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: You need to re-draw a game matrix.]
(c-1) What prices will the two companies now set in the Nash Equilibrium? [2 points]
(c-2) Compared to the situation in question (a), does Apple suffer or benefit from its capacity constraint, and by how much? What about Samsung? [2 points]

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have a unique NE that predicts the same outcome as in the sequential-move version of this game, where player 1 moves first? [6 points]
[Hint: find all possible values of X and Y; try to use backward induction without drawing the game tree for the sequential game.]

Section I: Short Questions [64 points]
Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.

	•	[8 points] The Italian Government is considering imposing a specific tax of 10 Euros (per unit sold) on Luxottica, a monopoly seller of sunglasses and fashion eyewear. What will be the effect of this tax on i) prices, ii) quantities and iii) consumer welfare? Explain your reasoning for each clearly and be as precise as possible.

Price will go up but not to the extent of 10 euros [3 points]
Quantities will go down [3 points]
Consumer welfare will go down [2 points]

	•	[8 points] The supply of ocean-breeding fish like cod and bluefish is highly volatile from year to year depending on the ocean currents. Despite this volatility in supply, cod and bluefish prices are relatively stable. What does this tell you? Illustrate with a graph. 

If the demand curve is very flat, which seems likely given the substitution possibilities, movements in the supply curve do little to change prices.
[Full credit once flat demand is mentioned. If someone talks about storing fish and ignores the demand curve give some points (2-5 points) for ingenuity.]
	•	[8 points] The market for first-flush black Darjeeling tea is perfectly competitive. Your analysis of the financial statements of a large Darjeeling tea producer reveals that the firm is making millions of dollars of profits. Is this consistent with your understanding that long-run economic profits of a firm in a perfectly competitive market should be zero? Provide a precise explanation for your answer.

[Full credit for one of the following explanations]
(1) Economic profits can be zero while accounting profits can still be positive. Financial statements show accounting profits – so ignores opportunity costs.
(2) When firms have economies of scale (e.g., when the ATC is U shaped), in the short run firms can earn a positive profit.
(3) Alternatively, marginal firms in a competitive market makes zero economic profit, but this firm is more efficient than the marginal ones.
	•	[8 points] A clawback provision is a special contractual clause sometimes included in employment contracts by financial firms, by which an executive may be required, under certain conditions, to pay back money already paid out. Explain precisely why you think such clauses exist. What specific economic problem do such provisions seek to address?
	•	

Addresses moral hazard. [5 points] Seeks to prevent managers from taking excessive risks or engaging in fraud that may be difficult to observe in the short run, but become apparent in the long run. [3 points]
	•	[8 points]  You have been hired to provide research help to a government agency investigating the pricing behavior of a monopolist.  Your regression work indicates that at its current prices, the demand elasticity facing this monopolist is -0.5.   Your boss looks at your results and tells you that she knows you are wrong.  Why does she think so?

A monopolist never produces on the inelastic part of his or her demand curve. [5 points] At an inelastic point the firm can increase price without losing many customers and thus increasing revenue. Or mentioning that the MR goes negative when the elasticity is < absolute value of 1, or being on the top half of a linear demand curve. [3 points]

	•	[8 points] Is price elasticity of demand higher in the short run or the long run? Explain your answer with an examples.

Demand is less elastic in short run. [5 points] Takes time to change behavior (example: Gasoline). [3 points]
[It is also ok if they say the opposite is true for some durable goods.]

	•	[8 points] A new Yale startup will be producing custom-tailored suits. The suits can be manufactured with a mix of inputs: machines (m), and employee labor (l). After some experimenting, the managers have determined how effective each of the two inputs are, and have computed the marginal products of the two inputs: MPm and MPl. They also know the prices of the two inputs, pm and pl. In a cost-minimizing production plan, what will be the relationship between those four objects, and why?
  
Accept either   or  (make sure subscripts are correct).   [4 points]
When this relationship holds, the firm has equalized the “return on investment” or “bang for the buck” from its purchase of inputs. Anything along those lines is fine. [4 points]


	•	[8 points] Publishers usually first sell the hardbound edition of a book, followed 12 to 18 months later with a the paperback edition of the same book. What do you think is the reason for this widespread practice? Why in your opinion do the two editions not come out at the same time?

This is intertemporal price discrimination (a special case of 2nd degree price discrimination) or self-selection. Publisher wants to separate two groups: the ones who are willing to spend money to read the book immediately and get the hardbound edition for their libraries, and the ones who just want to read the book (more price sensitive). [6 points] Without an interval, some from the first group may be tempted to buy a paperback edition and save some dollars. [2 points]

   1. [20 points] World Copper Market


       You have been hired by the American Bureau of Metal Statistics to analyze various metal

   6. [20 points] A game of add-on pricing

Consider a price competition between two movie theaters. They sell both movie tickets and popcorn.
Suppose they are offering the same film and the same type of popcorn. The cost of each movie ticket is
$20, and consumers’ willingness to pay is $30. The cost of each tub of popcorn is $2 and consumers’
willingness to pay is $8. Suppose the movie ticket price is publically observable but the popcorn price is
not (which is usually the case in the market). As a result, when consumers choose which theater to go,
they compare the ticket price only and always choose the cheaper one, but forget to take into account that
they will also buy popcorn once they arrive at a theater as long as its price does not exceed their
willingness to pay. We call such consumers “naïve consumers”. Suppose each consumer will buy at most
one tub of popcorn. Suppose the number of consumers is not big, so there is no need to worry about
capacity constraint. Suppose the two movie theaters choose their prices for the movie ticket and popcorn
simultaneously (and only once). If a consumer is indifferent between the two movie theaters, she will
randomly pick one.

                 a)   [8 points] What is the price of popcorn in the Nash equilibrium? What is the
                      price of the movie ticket in the Nash equilibrium? Explain your answers.?


        Since the price of popcorn does not affect consumers’ choice of where to go, popcorn will be sold
        at the monopoly price $8. [4 points]
        Ticket will be sold at a subsidized price $14 (=20-6), where 6 is the profit from selling the
        popcorn. That is, the profit from popcorn will be competed away in attracting consumers by
        setting a low ticket price. [4 points]

                 b)   [7 points] Suppose now that all the consumers become “savvy”: they find out the
                      popcorn prices and take them into account when they choose which movie
                      theater to go. Then what prices will the movie theaters set for their tickets and
                      popcorn, respectively, in the Nash equilibrium? What about if only half of the
                      consumers become “savvy”?

        If both prices are observable, one NE is $2 for popcorn and $20 for the ticket like in the standard
        Bertrand competition. [4 points. There are many other NE: any price combination with
        $x+$y=$22 is a NE. Give full credits for any right NE.]

        If only half of the consumers are savvy, then NE is $8 for popcorn and $14 for the ticket, the
        same as in question a). [3 points]
        [Here is the reason (but this is not needed for full credits): Savvy consumers will compare the
        bundle price, but the theaters are already making zero profit, so they cannot offer better prices.

Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Section I: Short Questions [70 points]
Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.

	•	[10 points] The Government of Malawi is planning to subsidize families that choose to take flu shots. Do you think this intervention would be beneficial or detrimental to society? Please provide a precise economic argument for your view.

Vaccines have positive externalities – this means people underestimate the benefit of a vaccine and therefore tend to under-consume. A subsidy can help alleviate this distortion.

5 points for noting positive externality leading to under-consumption
5 points for saying that subsidy can be beneficial

	•	[10 points] A study by the Haas Avocado Board notes that that there would likely be a 10% decrease in avocado crop because of an unusual heatwave in early July in California. The study further predicts that this 10% decrease in supply would drive avocado prices in the US up by as much as 30%, a remarkably large impact. From reading this study, what can you infer about the elasticity of demand for avocado - do you think demand for avocado is likely to be elastic or inelastic? Does this make sense intuitively? Why or why not?
 If a relatively small shift in the supply curve causes a big change in prices, this indicates that the demand curve must be quite steep. So you would expect demand for avocado to be inelastic.
This can make sense. Possible reasons: (i) The amount a household spends on avocado is likely very small as a proportion of total budget and so you can expect that people aren’t very price sensitive. (ii) Very important ingredient for many restaurants because there aren’t good substitutes (because it is a very popular item) – so they are less price sensitive.

5 points for noting that demand is steep or inelastic.
5 points for reasoning at least one reason for why inelastic demand makes sense

If student says “demand is elastic” but gives a sound explanation, give points for the second part. For example, if student says, demand is elastic and this makes sense because it is not a necessity.


	•	 [10 points] A branded drug is a pharmaceutical that is covered by a patent.  By law, only one firm can produce it, for a given length of time.  After that time patents expire, and when this happens, a generic drug can enter the marketplace.  The generic drug is chemically identical and is typically priced well below the price of the branded drug.  It is common to observe that when a generic drug is introduced into the marketplace, the price of the branded (e.g. previously patented) drug increases.  Explain why this might happen

The availability of cheaper substitutes will make some consumers move from using the branded product to using the generic. The consumers who continue to buy the branded drug are the less price elastic consumers. The branded firm now faces a customer segment that is less price sensitive. This means the firm with the branded drug can raise prices.
Give 5 points if they atleast recognize that some consumers shift to generic.
10 points for full argument.
	•	[10 points] A local company assembles machines using domestically-sourced steel and imported precision aluminum. They had already solved for an optimal mix of these inputs when the US dollar began to appreciate. As the US dollar strengthens, would you expect them to change their input mix towards using more steel, more aluminum, or continue with the same mix? Provide a formal economic reason for your answer,

8 points for saying that a stronger US dollar means imported aluminum is cheaper; they would move towards using more aluminum. 
Full credit if they mention that MPI of aluminum would increase, but MPI of steel stay the same. 


	•	[10 points] A television pundit claimed that while it is true that an import tariff is likely to increase prices to consumers, since the government collects that tariff revenue, the country is not any worse off. Do you agree or not, and why? 

The tariff introduces deadweight loss; the government revenue is less than the surplus destroyed. Therefore, no, the country is indeed worse off. If they mention DWL, full credit. 

	•	[10 points] Many executive compensation packages include “stock options” as part of the compensation scheme. What is the economic rationale for this type of compensation, as opposed to, say, granting shares or paying a fixed salary? 

The issue is that stock options are all “upside” and no downside. These “convexify” the CEO’s utility function. 
Full credit if students mention that these “correct” a risk-averse CEO’s incentives around the share price (make CEOs behave in the risk-neutral way that shareholders want them to, while pure share grants or salary don’t address risk aversion). 
Partial credit for “incentive problem” and “moral hazard”. 

	•	[10 points] Philadelphia, the nation's largest city with a soda tax levies a tax of 1.5-cents-per-ounce on the manufacturers of sweetened beverages. Analysts predict that the price of a 12 pack of 12-ounce sodas would increase by $2.16. Do you agree with this prediction? Argue why or why not.


No, the price would not go up by $2.16 (the full extent of the tax). This is because the incidence of tax is shared by manufacturers and consumers based on the relative elasticity of demand and supply. So tax will be partly borne by manufacturers too and price will go up by less than $2.16

Problem 6: A vaccine game (25 points)

Five Yale students are going on a foreign trip on which they will live close together. Where they are
going, there is a contagious disease which spreads easily among people who live close together. The value
of the trip to a student who does not contract the disease is 5. The value of the trip to a student who
contracts the disease is 0.

There is a vaccination against the disease. The vaccination costs different amounts for different students
(perhaps they have different health plans). Let’s call the students 1, 2,3,4 and 5 respectively. The
vaccination costs 1.1 for student 1, 2.1 for student 2, 3.1 for student 3, 4.1 for student 4, and 4.5 for
student 5.


If a student gets vaccinated, she will not contract the disease for sure. But, if she is not vaccinated then
her probability of getting the disease depends on the total number in the group who are not vaccinated.
(The probability is higher when more students are not vaccinated.) If she is the only person not to get
vaccinated then the probability that she gets the disease is 1/5 (e.g. from people outside of the group). If
there is one other person who is not vaccinated (i.e., two in all including her) then the probability that she
gets the disease is 2/5, etc. Finally if no students get vaccinated at all, she will get the disease for sure.


To make this into a game, suppose that each student aims to maximize her expected payoff and they are
risk neutral. If a student chooses to get vaccinated, her payoff is the value of a safe trip minus her cost of
vaccination. If a student chooses not to get vaccinated, her expected off is the probability of not getting

                                                       9
 
                                                                                                      Final Exam



the disease times the value of a safe trip. The students decide, individually and simultaneously, whether or
not to get a vaccination.



    a) (4 points) Is it a Nash Equilibrium (NE) that no students choose to get vaccinated? Please explain.
       No, it is not a NE. [2 points]
       With no vaccination at all, all students will get the disease. So for each student, they will have an
       individual incentive to get vaccinated given the benefit 5 is always greater their cost. [2 points; it
       is enough to mention one student has an incentive to deviate.]

    b) (4 points) Is it a NE that all students choose to get vaccinated? Please explain.
       No, it is not a NE. [2 points]
       Given all other students get vaccinated, each student has an individual incentive to not get
       vaccinated. For example, student 5’s payoff is 5-4.5=0.5 when she gets vaccinated, and this will
       increase to (4/5)*5=4 if she doesn’t get vaccinated. [2 points; as long as one deviating student is
       pointed out.]

    c) (7 points) Is it a NE that students 1, 2, 3 get vaccinated but students 4 and 5 do not? Please
       explain.
       No, it is not a NE. [2 points]
       Given the cost structure, we only need to consider students 3 and 4’s deviation incentives. Does
       student 3 want to deviate and not get vaccinated? Her payoff is now 5-3.1=1.9, and this will
       become (2/5)*5=2 if she individually switches to not get vaccinated (in which case the
       probability of being safe drops to 2/5). Therefore, student 3 will deviate. So the above is not a
       NE. (If we consider student 4, she actually has no incentive to deviate.) [5 points; no point if they
       use other students to show the deviation incentive.]

    d) (10 points) Is it a NE that students 1 and 2 get vaccinated but students 3, 4, 5 do not? Please
       explain.
       Yes, it is a NE. [2 points]
       Given the cost structure, now we only need to consider students 2 and 3’s deviation incentives.
       Does student 2 want to deviate and not get vaccinated? Her payoff is now 5-2.1=2.9, and this will
       become (1/5)*5=1 if she does not get vaccinated since the probability of being safe will drop to
       1/5. So student 2 does not want to deviate. [4 points]
       Does student 3 want to deviate and get vaccinated? Her payoff is now (2/5)*5=2, and if she
       deviates and gets vaccinated, her payoff will be 5-3.1=1.9. So student 3 does not want to deviate
       either. [4 points]

Problem 7: The game of hungry lions (15 points)

A lamb has just been thrown to four hungry lions. The lions are chained down in a line, with Lion 1
closest to the lamb and Lion 4 farthest from the lamb. Each lion’s chain is short enough that it can only
reach the two players immediately adjacent to it. The game proceeds as follows. Lion 1 first decides
whether or not to eat the lamb, and then lion 2 decides whether or not to eat Lion 1, and so on. If a lion

                                                     10
 
                                                                                                           Final Exam



eats anything, it will be then too heavy to defend itself so become vulnerable to attack. If a lion remains
hungry, the next lion will not attack it because a fight of two hungry lions would kill both. Each lion’s
preferences are fairly natural: best is to eat and stay alive, next best is to stay live but go hungry, next is to
eat and be eaten, and worst is to go hungry and be eaten (which wouldn’t happen here).



    a) (5 points) Will the lamb get eaten? Please explain.
       No, the lamb will survive. [2 points]
       Backward induction: L4 will eat if L3 has eaten; anticipating that, L3 won’t eat even if L2 has
       eaten; anticipating that, L2 will eat if L1 has eaten; then L1 shouldn’t eat in the beginning. [3
       points]

    b) (5 points) If there are 5 lions, will your answer to (a) change? Please explain.
       Now the lamb will get eaten. [2 points]
       The same backward induction: now L2 won’t eat even if L1 has eaten, so anticipating that L1 will
       eat the lamb. [3 points]

    c) (5 points) If there are 𝑁 lions in general (where 𝑁 is a positive integer), will the lamb get eaten?
       Please explain.
       It depends on whether N is an odd number or an even number. When it is an even number, we
       have the same result as in (a) and the lamb will survive. When it is an odd number, we have the
       same result as in (b) and the lamb will get eaten. [full points once they point out whether N is
       even or odd matters.]

                      i.e. -12+18P=32.4-5.4P

                      think about how price changes along the same demand curve affect demand.

                      implies 22.95P=43.8
                      implies P=1.95 [2 points]
                      and Q=21.88 [1 point]

[1 point for each number in the cost column; 2 points for each number in the premium column]

                      competitive so that premiums equal average cost, what would the premium be?

The average cost is (1/3)*($1250+$2500+$5000)=$2917. This is the actuarially fair premium.


                 c)   [10 points] If insurance were optional, and again insurance is competitive so that
                      premiums equal average cost, what premium would insurers charge? And which
                      risk types would choose to buy insurance at that premium? (Hint: Note that they
                      would consider charging one of the values solved for in part a. If they charged
                      the lowest, all three types would buy; if they charge the 2nd lowest, two types
                      would buy; if they charged the highest, only one type would buy).

        If the insurer prices at $1335 or below, its average cost will be
        (1/3)*($1250+$2500+$5000)=$2917. Negative profit.

        If the insurer prices between $1336 and $2907, its average cost will be
        (1/2)*($2500+$5000)=$3750. Negative profit.

        If the insurer prices between $2908 and $9750, its average cost will be $5000.

So it can avoid negative profit if it serves HIGH types only, at an actuarially fair premium of $5000.

   6. [20 points] A game of add-on pricing

Consider a price competition between two movie theaters. They sell both movie tickets and popcorn.
Suppose they are offering the same film and the same type of popcorn. The cost of each movie ticket is
$20, and consumers’ willingness to pay is $30. The cost of each tub of popcorn is $2 and consumers’
willingness to pay is $8. Suppose the movie ticket price is publically observable but the popcorn price is
not (which is usually the case in the market). As a result, when consumers choose which theater to go,
they compare the ticket price only and always choose the cheaper one, but forget to take into account that
they will also buy popcorn once they arrive at a theater as long as its price does not exceed their
willingness to pay. We call such consumers “naïve consumers”. Suppose each consumer will buy at most
one tub of popcorn. Suppose the number of consumers is not big, so there is no need to worry about
capacity constraint. Suppose the two movie theaters choose their prices for the movie ticket and popcorn
simultaneously (and only once). If a consumer is indifferent between the two movie theaters, she will
randomly pick one.

                 a)   [8 points] What is the price of popcorn in the Nash equilibrium? What is the
                      price of the movie ticket in the Nash equilibrium? Explain your answers.?


        Since the price of popcorn does not affect consumers’ choice of where to go, popcorn will be sold
        at the monopoly price $8. [4 points]
        Ticket will be sold at a subsidized price $14 (=20-6), where 6 is the profit from selling the
        popcorn. That is, the profit from popcorn will be competed away in attracting consumers by
        setting a low ticket price. [4 points]

                 b)   [7 points] Suppose now that all the consumers become “savvy”: they find out the
                      popcorn prices and take them into account when they choose which movie
                      theater to go. Then what prices will the movie theaters set for their tickets and
                      popcorn, respectively, in the Nash equilibrium? What about if only half of the
                      consumers become “savvy”?

        If both prices are observable, one NE is $2 for popcorn and $20 for the ticket like in the standard
        Bertrand competition. [4 points. There are many other NE: any price combination with
        $x+$y=$22 is a NE. Give full credits for any right NE.]

        If only half of the consumers are savvy, then NE is $8 for popcorn and $14 for the ticket, the
        same as in question a). [3 points]
        [Here is the reason (but this is not needed for full credits): Savvy consumers will compare the
        bundle price, but the theaters are already making zero profit, so they cannot offer better prices.

Problem 1. Boycotts

Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM
economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the
instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We
advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer
yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own.

Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union
involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge
all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the
resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions.
However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small.

In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of
boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in
quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic
of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the
problem with calculation -- rethink the economics.

Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in
question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous
buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on
the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott,
do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in
particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into
observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those
that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any
circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For
simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every
price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price.

    Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by:

                                           𝑄   = 5000 − 10𝑃

where 𝑄 = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with 𝑄 =
quantity supplied):

                                           𝑄 = 50 (𝑃 − 5)

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero.

Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation.
 
    Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied:

                                     𝑄 = 𝑄
                                     5000 – 10𝑃 = 50 (𝑃 − 5)
                                     60𝑃 = 5250
                                     𝑃 = 87.5
                                     𝑄 = 4125




Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the
boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers?


       Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing
       target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for
       demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target
       sellers is:

                              𝑄   = 0.3 (5000 − 10𝑃) = 1500 − 3𝑃



Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

                               Observers as Fraction           Target as Fraction
       Situation               of Original Demand              of Original Supply

         A                             3/4                             3/4

         B                             1/4                             1/4

         C                             1/2                             1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott
conditions. Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target
and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to
the observance fraction and the target fraction. “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the
price to target sellers. Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the
remaining problems. What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an
understanding of simple economic principles. The point of the exercise is to determine whether the
boycott is effective in any of the three cases above. To answer this question, you have been given
 
the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the
market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers. Items (1) and (2) imply that it is
possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted
sellers. You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-
market.

Problem 2. Black quinoa in Bolivia

The Government of Bolivia is planning to impose a price control in the market for black quinoa, an
important part of the standard diet of people in the country. Market demand for black quinoa is
given by 𝑄 = 40 – 𝑃 and the market supply curve is given by 𝑄 = −20 + 3𝑃, where P denotes
the market price in dollars per kilogram and 𝑄 and 𝑄 denote the quantities demanded and
supplied, respectively (measured in millions of kilograms per year).

   a. What is the equilibrium market price and quantity? What is the consumer surplus and the
      producer surplus?

           a. Answer: 40-P=-20+3P

               P=15

               So, Q=25

               C.S.=(1/2)*25*25=312.25

               P.S.=(1/2)*25*(25/3)=104.17



   b. Find the elasticity of demand at the equilibrium price? Is the demand for quinoa elastic or
      inelastic? Is this elasticity consistent with what you expected? Explain

           a. Answer: Elasticity= -15/25=-0.6

               So demand is inelastic which is consistent with quinoa being an essential part of the
               Bolivian diet.



   c. Consider the situation where the Government imposes a price control limiting market price
      to no higher than $10. What is the new equilibrium price and quantity? What are the
      resulting consumer surplus, producer surplus and total surplus?

           a. Answer: New price is $10

               New quantity is 10

               C.S=(1/2)*10*10+20*10=250

               P.S.=(1/2)*10*(10/3)=16.67

               Total Surplus=266.67
 
d. Looking at the effect of the price control on the total surplus, what would you say? Is this an
   efficient step? Why or why not?

       a. Answer: This was not an efficient step since total social surplus went down.



e. What if the government limited the market price to $15.50? Would this be a smart move?
   Why or why not?

       a. Answer: A price control of $15.50 would not have any effect since the equilibrium
          price is already below $15.50.

Problem 1. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber

a.      Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber
is given by

                                          𝑄 = 40 − 2𝑃
       where 𝑄 is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound.
       Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is
       given by
                                                 2
                                            𝑄 = 𝑃
                                                 3
       where 𝑄 is US output in millions of pounds and P is the domestic price per pound.

       Solve for the domestic (autarky) equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

       Price: _____________________

       Quantity: _____________________


       Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.


   b. Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound.
       Suppose the US opens up to free trade and there is enough availability that the US can
       import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? If so,
       write the quantity that will be imported. If not, enter “0”.

       Quantity imported: ________________________



       Solution: Demand would be 22, supply would be 6, so 16 would be imported.


   c. Subsequently, the US government imposed a $9 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you
      predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?


       US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________

       Solution: This would raise the effective world price to $18, and the US would no longer
       import any fiber. The price would be the autarky price of 15. (They would not become an
       exporter, as the true world price remains at 9).


   d. What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?

       Tariff revenue: ________________________

       Solution: 0, since none is imported.
 
e. Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers
   from such a tariff?

     Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     US domestic consumer surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Total US domestic surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be
     worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce
     deadweight loss.


f.   (Challenge question, diagram helps here): Under pressure from trading partners, the US is
     considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US
     to 8 million pounds a year (𝑄 = 8). Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the
     equilibrium domestic price of vegetable fiber (understanding that the quota would lead to
     upward price pressure if the quota is binding)? What will be the amount produced by
     domestic producers?


     Answer.

     To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be
     met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds).
 
        So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, we will have domestic demand
        outstrip domestic supply by 8 million pounds.

        Domestic price: _____________________

        Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________


        40-2P=8+2/3PP=12

        Domestic Q=8

MGT 404 2018                                                                                          Final Exam



Section I: Short Questions [70 points]
Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.


1) [10 points] The Government of Malawi is planning to subsidize families that choose to take flu
   shots. Do you think this intervention would be beneficial or detrimental to society? Please
   provide a precise economic argument for your view.




2) [10 points] A study by the Haas Avocado Board notes that that there would likely be a 10%
   decrease in avocado crop because of an unusual heatwave in early July in California. The study
   further predicts that this 10% decrease in supply would drive avocado prices in the US up by as
   much as 30%, a remarkably large impact. From reading this study, what can you infer about the
   elasticity of demand for avocado - do you think demand for avocado is likely to be elastic or
   inelastic? Does this make sense intuitively? Why or why not?




3)    [10 points] A branded drug is a pharmaceutical that is covered by a patent. By law, only one
     firm can produce it, for a given length of time. After that time patents expire, and when this
     happens, a generic drug can enter the marketplace. The generic drug is chemically identical
     and is typically priced well below the price of the branded drug. It is common to observe that
     when a generic drug is introduced into the marketplace, the price of the branded (e.g.
     previously patented) drug increases. Explain why this might happen




4)    [10 points] A local company assembles machines using domestically-sourced steel and
     imported precision aluminum. They had already solved for an optimal mix of these inputs when
     the US dollar began to appreciate. As the US dollar strengthens, would you expect them to

Problem 6: Extended location games [25 points]
Recall the location choice game discussed in the class: Tourists are evenly distributed on a beach that is one mile long. Two ice cream vendors simultaneously choose where to locate, each aiming to maximize their own market share. (Suppose they can locate exactly at the same spot if they want.) Tourists prefer to buy from the closer vendor. When they are indifferent between vendors, they randomly choose one. In the following, we consider a few variants of this location choice game.
Suppose that there are now THREE ice cream vendors.
(a) [5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that they all locate in the middle of the beach? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. 
When they all locate in the middle, each vendor has a market share 1/3. But if a vendor individually deviates from the middles slightly, its market share can be close to 1/2. 
(b) [5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that they locate at 1/6, 1/2, and 5/6, respectively (so that each has a market share 1/3)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. 
The vendor at 1/6 or 5/6 has a unilateral incentive to move toward the vendor in the middle. (The vendor at the middle actually has NO strict incentive to move.) 
(c) [5 points] Suppose now we “bend” the beach so that it becomes a CIRCLE that has a circumference of one mile (i.e., the two endpoints 0 and 1 are now connected to each other). Is the location configuration in question (b) a Nash equilibrium? Please explain your answer.
Yes, it is now a NE. 
Given the other two vendors’ locations, a vendor’s market share cannot exceed 1/3 no matter where it moves to. 
Let us now return to the original location game with only TWO ice cream vendors and a LINEAR beach.
(d) [5 points] Suppose now each tourist never travels more than 1/4 mile to buy an ice cream. That is, if the distance to either vendor is longer than 1/4 mile, a tourist will not buy. What are the Nash equilibria now?
The only Nash equilibrium is that one vendor at 1/4 and the other at 3/4.
(e) [5 points] Suppose now each tourist never travels more than 1/5 mile to buy an ice cream. How will your answer change?
One Nash equilibrium is that one vendor at 1/5 and the other at 4/5. But now there are many other Nash equilibria. For example, one at 1/4 and the other at 3/4. In fact, any configuration is a NE where the two vendors are at least 2/5 mile away from each other and their distance to the closer endpoint is at least 1/5 mile.

Problem 7: Investment game [20 points]
Consider the following sequential-move game: firm 1 first decides whether to adopt an aggressive low-price policy or an accommodating high-price policy; after seeing firm 1’s choice, firm 2 decides whether or not to invest in a new technology which reduces its production cost. The investment cost is k.
If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, then firm 1 will earn 5, and firm 2 will have no business (e.g., because its production cost is too high) and earn 0. If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 invests to reduce its production cost, then firm 1 earns 1 and firm 2 earns 4 (before considering its investment cost). If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, firm 1 will earn 4, and firm 2 will have some business and earn 2. If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 invests, then firm 1 will have no business and earn 0, and firm 2 will earn 5 (before considering its investment cost).
(a) [5 points] Draw the game tree of this sequential-move game. [Hint: payoffs are net profit, so don’t forget the investment cost.]


(b) [10 points] For what values of k, will firm 1 choose to set a high price in the beginning?
k should be between 3 and 4.
Two conditions should be satisfied for firm 1 to set a high price: First, when firm 1 sets a high price, firm 2 should respond by choosing not to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 0, which must be worse than choosing a low price.) This requires 5-k<2, or k>3. Second, when firm 1 sets a low price, firm 2 should respond by choosing to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 5, in which case it wouldn’t choose a high price.) This requires 4-k>0, or k<4.
(c) [5 points] Suppose the investment cost increases from k=2.5 to k=3.5. Will firm 2 suffer or benefit from that? Please explain your answer.
Firm 2 benefits from the increase of k. 
When k=2.5, firm 1 will set a low price, and then firm 2 will invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 1.5. When k=3.5, firm 1 will set a high price, and then firm 2 will not invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 2. (A higher investment cost induces firm 1 to be less aggressive in the beginning.)

         favorable climate, Mexico is able to supply avocados at a price of $1 per pound (and there is
         effectively no limit on how many can be grown).

          If there is free trade with Mexico, how many millions of pounds of avocados will be
          consumed in the US, and what share of them will be produced in the US? Are US producers
          and consumers better or worse off from trade?

      c. The government is considering imposing a tariff on imported avocados to help US farmers.
         Suppose they were to levy a tariff of $0.20 per pound on imports. By how much would US
         consumer surplus increase or decrease from this tariff? By how much would US producer
         surplus increase or decrease from this tariff? How much government revenue would the
         tariff generate? (Hint: draw a diagram of the situation and compute areas corresponding to
         the different objects at the free-trade equilibrium and tariff equilibrium).

          Change in US consumer surplus from tariff: _____________________

          Change in US producer surplus from tariff: _____________________

          Government revenue from tariff: _____________________

          Answer. Price will increase to $1.20, as this is still less than the no-trade price. The new
          equilibrium has 𝑄 = 280, 𝑄 = 100. Consumer surplus goes from (0.5)(3)(300) =
          450 𝑡𝑜 (0.5)(2.8)(280) = 392, for a change of −58. Producer surplus goes from
          (0.5)(0.6)(75) = 22.5 𝑡𝑜 (0.5)(0.8)(100) = 40, for a change of +17.5. Tariff revenue would
          be $0.2 times the quantity imported (which is quantity demanded less domestic supply), or
          (180)(0.2) = 36.



      d. Avocado lovers are not happy about the plan and decide to lobby against it. The government
         proposes to hand back 100% of the tariff revenue it collects to avocado consumers at the
         end of the year.2 Would this make avocado lovers just as well-off as under free trade? Why?

          □ Yes

          □ No

       Answer. No, it would not, as the tariff revenue (36) is less than the change in consumer
       surplus (-58). This is the key insight: even total surplus falls from the tariff.

Problem 3. Car Pricing

  Hyundai is developing an all-new, premium, low-cost electric car to compete with
  Tesla’s Model 3. They plan to market the vehicle in both the US (“U”) and Asia (“A”).
  Demand is estimated to be
                                   𝑄 = 4,000,000 − 100𝑃
                                   𝑄 = 6,000,000 − 200𝑃

  They estimate that they will be able to produce the car at a constant marginal cost of
  $25,000 in either location.

            a)   First, assume it is very costly to ship cars across the Pacific, and so
                 the company is able to charge different prices in different parts of the
                 world. What is the optimal price, quantity, and profit level in each
                 market?

                 US Price: ___________________

                 US Quantity: ___________________
 
                US Profit: ___________________

                Asia Price: ___________________

                Asia Quantity: ___________________

                Asia Profit: ___________________

                                       𝑈𝑆: 𝑃 = 40,000 − 0.01𝑄
                                       𝑀𝑅 = 40,000 − 0.02𝑄
                                       0.02𝑄 = 15000
                                       𝑄 = 750,000
                                       𝑃 = 32,500
                                    𝜋 = (32500 − 25000) ∗ 750000
                                    = 5,625𝑀
                ASIA
                                     𝑃 = 30,000 − 0.005𝑄
                                     𝑀𝑅 = 30,000 − 0.01𝑄
                                     25000 = 30000 − 0.01𝑄
                                     0.01𝑄 = 5000
                                     𝑄 = 500,000
                                     𝑃 = $27,500
                                     𝜋 = (2500 ∗ 500000) = 1,250𝑀


           b)   The company is concerned is very concerned about its image in the
                US and is worried about a backlash from consumers if they charge
                more in one market than another. What is the optimal price, quantity
                and profit level if they are constrained to charge the same price in
                both markets? (Note, answers may not be round numbers – you may
                round price to the nearest $100 and profit to the nearest $1M).

                          𝑄 = 10,000,000 − 300𝑃
                          𝑃 = 33,333.33 − 0.00333𝑄
                          𝑀𝑅 = 33,333.33 − 0.006666𝑄
                          25000 = 33,333.33 − 0.0066666𝑄
                          8333.33 = 0.006666𝑞
                          𝑞 = 1.25𝑀
                          𝑝 = $29,167
                          𝜋 = (4167 ∗ 1250000) = 5,209𝑀
BUT – this is worse than just serving the US market! (e.g. the optimal price is above
the kink in the demand curve)
 
SO – charge $32,500 everywhere, have no sales in Asia, make 5.625B, sell 750,000



          c)   The company has determined that they could pay $100M to make the
               US version sufficiently different, with an entirely different marketing
               campaign, such that they could charge different prices in the two
               different markets. Is this a good idea for the firm to do?

          Just charge the same price everywhere

          Charge different prices in Europe and the US, incur $100M cost

          Not sure / Not enough information.

Answer: Yes, being able to earn $1.250B in the Asian market is worth $100M
